coredata.dc:title: Alfalfa biomass as a green source for the synthesis of N,S-CDs via microwave treatment. Application as a nano sensor for nifuroxazide in formulations and gastric juice
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Osman, Mohamed M.
El-Shaheny, Rania
Ibrahim, Fawzia A.
coredata.dc:description:
Background Researchers have investigated different techniques for synthesis of carbon dots. These techniques include Arc discharge, laser ablation, oxidation, water/solvothermal, and chemical vapor deposition. However, these techniques suffer from some limitations like the utilization of gaseous charged particles, high current, high temperature, potent oxidizing agents, non-environmentally friendly carbon sources, and the generation of uneven particle size. Therefore, there was a significant demand for the adoption of a new technology that combines the environmentally friendly aspects of both bio-based carbon sourcing and synthesis technique.
Results
Medicago sativa L (alfalfa)-derived N, S-CDs have been successfully synthesized via microwave irradiation. The N,S-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence (λ ex/em of 320/420 nm) with fluorescence quantum yield of 2.2 % and high-water solubility. The produced N,S-CDs were characterized using TEM, EDX, Zeta potential analysis, IR, UV–Visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The average diameter of the produced N, S-CDs was 4.01 ± 1.2 nm, and the Zeta potential was −24.5 ± 6.63 mv. The stability of the produced nano sensors was also confirmed over wide pH range, long time, and in presence of different ions. The synthesized N, S-CDs were employed to quantify the antibacterial drug, nifuroxazide (NFZ), by fluorescence quenching via inner filter effect mechanism. The method was linear with NFZ concentration ranging from 1.0 to 30.0 μM. LOD and LOQ were 0.16 and 0.49 μM, respectively. The method was applied to quantify NFZ in simulated gastric juice (SGJ) with % recovery 99.59 ± 1.4 in addition to pharmaceutical dosage forms with % recovery 98.75 ± 0.61 for Antinal Capsules® and 100.63 ± 1.54 for Antinal suspension®. The Method validation was performed in compliance with the criteria outlined by ICH.
Significance and novelty The suggested approach primarily centers on the first-time use of alfalfa, an ecologically sustainable source of dopped-CDs, and a cost-effective synthesis technique via microwave irradiation, which is characterized by low energy consumption, minimized reaction time, and the ability to control the size of the produced CDs. This is in line with the growing global recognition of the implementation of green analytical chemistry principles.

head.ce:title: Alfalfa biomass as a green source for the synthesis of N,S-CDs via microwave treatment. Application as a nano sensor for nifuroxazide in formulations and gastric juice

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Researchers have investigated different techniques for synthesis of carbon dots. These techniques include Arc discharge, laser ablation, oxidation, water/solvothermal, and chemical vapor deposition. However, these techniques suffer from some limitations like the utilization of gaseous charged particles, high current, high temperature, potent oxidizing agents, non-environmentally friendly carbon sources, and the generation of uneven particle size. Therefore, there was a significant demand for the adoption of a new technology that combines the environmentally friendly aspects of both bio-based carbon sourcing and synthesis technique.

Medicago sativa L (alfalfa)-derived N, S-CDs have been successfully synthesized via microwave irradiation. The N,S-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence (λ ex/em of 320/420 nm) with fluorescence quantum yield of 2.2 % and high-water solubility. The produced N,S-CDs were characterized using TEM, EDX, Zeta potential analysis, IR, UV–Visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The average diameter of the produced N, S-CDs was 4.01 ± 1.2 nm, and the Zeta potential was −24.5 ± 6.63 mv. The stability of the produced nano sensors was also confirmed over wide pH range, long time, and in presence of different ions. The synthesized N, S-CDs were employed to quantify the antibacterial drug, nifuroxazide (NFZ), by fluorescence quenching via inner filter effect mechanism. The method was linear with NFZ concentration ranging from 1.0 to 30.0 μM. LOD and LOQ were 0.16 and 0.49 μM, respectively. The method was applied to quantify NFZ in simulated gastric juice (SGJ) with % recovery 99.59 ± 1.4 in addition to pharmaceutical dosage forms with % recovery 98.75 ± 0.61 for Antinal Capsules® and 100.63 ± 1.54 for Antinal suspension®. The Method validation was performed in compliance with the criteria outlined by ICH.

The suggested approach primarily centers on the first-time use of alfalfa, an ecologically sustainable source of dopped-CDs, and a cost-effective synthesis technique via microwave irradiation, which is characterized by low energy consumption, minimized reaction time, and the ability to control the size of the produced CDs. This is in line with the growing global recognition of the implementation of green analytical chemistry principles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are well-established carbonaceous nanomaterial that possess unique features and diverse applications. CDs show sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon core and particle size usually less than 10 nm [1]. The distinctive configuration and dimensions of CDs imply the potential for adjustable chemical, physical, optical, and electrical characteristics [2]. CDs exhibit low toxicity, distinctive luminescent capabilities, high solubility in water, thermal stability, chemical inertness, and ease of functionalization with heteroatoms such as sulfur, nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus [3]. The diverse characteristics demonstrated by CDs render them very suitable for implementation in several domains, including but not limited to photoelectronic applications, optical sensing, pharmaceutical analysis, biosensing, biomedicine, and catalysis [4,5].

Since the first discovery of carbon dots (CDs), significant research has been dedicated to investigating novel approaches for their synthesis, aiming to achieve simplicity, high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness of CDs sourcing [6]. Recently, there has been an increasing focus on the green synthesis of CDs, which involves employing recyclable materials, crops and their residue, waste from industries, and household trash [7]. The utilization of sustainable biomass or biodegradable waste as precursors for CDs has numerous advantages, such as straightforward operation, easy availability of raw materials, and the promotion of waste reduction [8]. Besides, the conversion of low-value crops or wastes into high-value CDs contributes to the advancement of sustainable materials and circular economy concept via decreasing raw materials consumption and increasing materials life span. However, to complement the idea of green chemistry, it is mandatory to develop an environmentally friendly method for the synthesis of CDs. This will help build a solid paradigm merging green chemistry and circular economy [9].

In this context, the reported literature revealed several frequently utilized techniques for CDs synthesis. The Arc discharge method is considered the earliest technique for synthesizing CDs. However, the components of this method are complicated due to the utilization of gaseous charged particles, strong current, and high temperature, thereby rendering the isolation and purification of the synthesized CDs a challenging task [10]. The laser ablation synthesis method involves the fast application of laser energy to carbon target dispersed in organic or non-organic solvents. However, this approach has certain drawbacks. Specifically, the produced CDs typically require subsequent treatments such as irradiation, oxidation, and passivation in addition to uneven particle size distribution [11]. Laser ablation in deionized water is preferred due to its cost-effectiveness, safety, and high heat capacity in addition, the synthesized CDs may form oxide or hydroxides through plasma induced breakdown of water molecules and the hydroxyl group has the ability to be taken up by the surface of the ablated nanoparticles, resulting in their electrostatic stabilization and good purity [12]. The nanoparticles that have undergone ablation in an organic solvent, such as ethanol, exhibit instability and a higher level of aggregation due to the decreased polarity of the solvent. Also, laser light absorption causes the breakdown of organic solvents, resulting in the creation of air bubbles which obstruct the laser path and reduce the purity of the generated CDs [12]. Furthermore, chemical oxidation method for CDs synthesis involves oxidizing a carbon precursor using a potent oxidizing agent. However, a significant drawback encountered by this method is the lack of uniformity in the size distribution of the produced CDs in addition to the use of harsh chemicals and conditions [13]. Besides, the electrochemical oxidation method has limited applicability due to the involvement of intricate processing steps and the utilization of expensive electrodes [14]. The acid oxidation method primarily involves subjecting the carbon source to acid treatment. However, the disadvantages associated with this method are the challenge of achieving effective separation, the post-treatment passivation, the limit of control over particle size, the need of vigorous reaction process, and the requirement of extreme circumstances [15]. In Water/Solvothermal synthesis method the CDs are prepared through mixing the carbon source with solvent, followed by heating in an autoclave or reactor. However, this method presents certain limitations, including the utilization of hazardous organic solvents, the requirement for exceptionally high temperatures, reaching up to 300 °C, and extended time durations, potentially exceeding 24 h [16]. The template synthesis method is also used; however, certain templates express challenges in their separation from CDs. Consequently, the fluorescence properties of CDs may be compromised during the acid-base washing or heating procedures employed for template removal [17]. Additionally, chemical vapor deposition method is used for CDs synthesis, however, the use of high temperature up to 1000°C limits its application [18]. Thus, in conjunction with the increasing global recognition of the implementation of green analytical chemistry principles, the strong need for a safe, eco-friendly, fast, and easily implemented synthesis method is obvious.

The proposed study mainly emphasizes the utilization of environmentally sustainable sourcing of dopped-CDs in addition to cost-effective fast synthesis technique via microwave irradiation which is characterized by low energy consumption, minimized time of reaction, and the capability of controlling the size of the produced CDs. N,S-CDs were synthesized for the first time using a one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis of alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) biomass at 700 W for 14 min only. The green leafy alfalfa hay is used as a nutrient for livestock as it contains approximately 20 % protein encompassing all essential amino acids necessary for nutritional purposes, including those containing sulfur, such as methionine and cysteine [19]. Besides, alfalfa is an excellent source of several vitamins and minerals where 1 cup of alfalfa contains vitamin K 8 % of the daily value (DV), iron 2 %, vitamin C 3 %, thiamine 2 %, magnesium 2 %, folate 3 %, riboflavin 3 %, and cupper 6 %. In addition to containing 1 g of protein and 1 g of carbohydrates [20]. Alfalfa possesses a substantial concentration of bioactive phytochemicals, such as saponins, coumarins, flavonoids, phytosterols, phytoestrogens, and alkaloids [21]. Moreover, its remarkable ability to regenerate new stems and leaves after cutting allows for multiple hay harvests, with up to 8 crops being obtained within a single growing season [22]. These characteristics render it an exceptionally sustainable, renewable, environmentally friendly, and economically viable carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur source. The presence of carbon, nitrogen and sulfur in alfalfa implies the potential for self-doping of CDs with nitrogen and sulfur through microwave assisted pyrolysis. These prominent features of alfalfa highlighted its advantages as a source for CDs synthesis over different substrates those are utilized currently such as chemical precursors like m-phenylenediamine and citric acid [23], citric acid and l-histidine [24], urea [25], and bio-based precursors like spirulina algae [26], spirulina algae and l-arginine [27], citrus fruit [22], red beetroot [28], and cantaloupe waste [29].

The synthesized alfalfa-derived N,S-CDs exhibited robust fluorescence, exceptional water solubility, and remarkable stability, making them suitable for use as an excellent fluorescence sensor in pharmaceutical and biological analysis. In this study, we effectively utilized the synthesized N,S-CDs to quantitatively analyze the antidiarrheal medication nifuroxazide (NFZ) in pharmaceutical formulations. Nifuroxazide 2’-(5-Nitrofurfurylidene)-4-hydroxybenzohydrazide (Fig. 6B) is a commonly employed antibacterial agent for the treatment of colitis and diarrhea with limited absorption capacity within the gastrointestinal system [30]. Recent studies reported that NFZ has a variety of other pharmacological actions, such as its ability to combat cancer, acting as an antioxidant, and reducing inflammation [31]. NFZ has the potential to inhibit various types of malignancies, including thyroid, breast, lung, bladder, liver, and colon cancers, as well as osteosarcoma and melanoma [31]. Reviewing literature revealed the presence of different analytical methodologies for the determination of NFZ, encompassing spectrofluorimetry [32–34], spectrophotometry [35–38], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [35,38], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [38,39]. These approaches are characterized by their time-consuming nature, necessitating costly equipment, involving laborious pre-analysis procedures, and employing potentially hazardous organic solvents. Hence, there is a significant demand for a straightforward and environmentally friendly methodology to analyze NFZ in different pharmaceutical dosage forms. The utilization of microwave-assisted synthesis to produce N,S-CDs as a fluorescent sensor, employing alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) as an affordable, naturally occurring, and sustainable source of carbon, nitrogen and sulfur, exhibits enhanced efficiency, simplicity, environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and economic viability when compared to previously documented methodologies. Moreover, this analytical method showed sensitivity and specificity in determination of NFZ in commercially available pharmaceutical dosage forms.

Pure powder form of Nifuroxazide was provided by EVA Pharmaceutical Company, located in Cairo, Egypt. Antinal® capsules, manufactured by Amoun Pharmaceutical Company in Cairo, Egypt, contain 200 mg of NFZ per each capsule and Antinal® suspension produced by Amoun Pharmaceutical Company (Cairo, Egypt) contains 220 mg of NFZ per 5 mL were bought from a nearby pharmacy. Delta Pharma S.A.E, located in the 10th of Ramadan City in Egypt, supplied spiramycin pure powder. European Egyptian Pharmaceuticals Co. (Alexandria, Egypt) provided aceclofenac pure powder. Alexandria Co. for pharmaceuticals and chemical industries (Alexandria, Egypt) provided dexamethasone, while EIPICO Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt) provided paracetamol pure powder. Quinine sulphate and pepsin from porcine gastric mucosa (powder 250 units solid/mg) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. louis MO, USA).

Alfalfa was obtained from a farm positioned in Damietta, Egypt. Citric acid, sucrose, hydrochloric acid, and sodium hydroxide were supplied by El Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Sodium citrate and sodium chloride were obtained from PIOCHEM for laboratory chemicals (6th of October City, Egypt). Magnesium sulphate, calcium chloride, potassium chloride, and carboxy methyl cellulose were bought from OXFORD LAB FINE CHEM LLP (Maharashtra, India). Glucose was purchased from ALPHA CHEMIKA (Maharashtra, India). Za Chem Company supplied acetic acid with a purity of 99.7 % and orthophosphoric acid with a purity of 85 %. Ferrous sulphate was supplied by DIACHEM, and ferric chloride was purchased from NATCO for laboratory chemicals. Fisher Scientific Co. (USA) supplied methanol of HPLC grade and boric acid. All chemicals chosen for the experiment were of analytical high standards, in addition to utilization of distilled water for simplicity.

The fluorescence readings were recorded using an Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, United States) Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The instrument was equipped with a Xenon flash lamp and a 1 cm quartz cuvette. The measurements were performed at a voltage of 800 V, a scan speed of 600 nm/min, and excitation/emission monochromator's slit widths of 5 nm. The spectrophotometric measurements were conducted using the JENWAY 6850 UV–Vis spectrophotometer manufactured by Cole Parmer in the United Kingdom. The N, S-CDs were imaged using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) manufactured in Tokyo, Japan. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) analysis was performed with FTIR spectroscopy ALPHA II instrument (Bruker Co., Germany). The zeta potential of N,S-CDs was measured using the Malvern Zetasizer, a device made by Malvern Panalytical, Ltd, located in Worcestershire, UK. The analysis of elements was carried out by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis on a scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an Oxford X-Max 20 detector. The experimental setup also included the use of other laboratory equipment including Jac NGM-2002 microwave oven, which had a capacity of 20 L and a power output of 700 W, a centrifuge of model 2–16P, manufactured in Germany, and 3510 pH-meter (JENWAY, Cole Parmer, UK). The Wisd Wise Clean WUC A22H Ultrasonic Bath, produced by Witeg in Germany, was also utilized. Lastly, the Water Still DESA 0081 Vidrio Industrial POBEL, made in Spain, was used.

To prepare a 1.0 mM standard solution of NFZ, a precise quantity of NFZ pure powder (13.76 mg) was carefully measured and added to a 50 mL volumetric flask. Absolute methanol was used for completion of the flask final volume to obtain the desired concentration. The working solution of NFZ was generated by transferring 5 mL of the standard solution into a 25 mL volumetric flask, followed by dilution with distilled water to get 0.2 mM solution of NFZ. Solution of N,S-CDS was prepared by diluting 1000 μL of the prepared N,S-CDS with distilled water in a 25 mL volumetric flask.

The process of synthesis of N,S-CDs using microwave-assisted synthesis technique is shown in Fig. 1 . Freshly collected alfalfa leaves and stems were carefully gathered, thoroughly cleansed and washed using distilled water. The washed leaves and stems were subsequently thoroughly shaken up and down before being exposed to a fan for a duration of 15 min to ensure complete removal of residual water droplets. Finally, they were fragmented into smaller pieces. These pieces were thoroughly grounded with a household fruit mixer until a homogenous paste was obtained. A quantity of 10 g from the acquired paste was weighed in a Pyrex glass beaker and subjected to a microwave treatment for 14 min at 700 W until formation of a dry charred matrix. The matrix was permitted to reach ambient temperature before being scratched and finely grinded using a mortar and pestle. The product was mixed with 20 mL of distilled water and subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min. The dark brown solution underwent centrifugation for 20 min at 5000 rpm to eliminate any insoluble detritus present. A filtration process was conducted on the resultant supernatant using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. The N,S-CDs solution was stored at a temperature of 4 °C in a glass container with an amber color to preserve it until later usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield for the synthesized N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The determination of the quantum yield (Ф) of the synthesized N,S-CDs was carried out by comparing it to quinine sulphate, which was prepared in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution and had a fluorescence quantum yield of 54 % [40]. In order to lessen the impact of the inner filter effect (IFE), solutions of quinine sulphate and N,S-CDs were prepared ensuring that their absorbance values were below 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (λ ex). The quantum yield (Ф) was determined by employing eq. (1) for calculation [40]:
(1)

In the above equation, Ф represents the quantum yield, F denotes the integrated fluorescence, A signifies the absorbance, and n represents the refractive index of the solvent. The symbols “St” and “x" are used to denote standard quinine sulphate and N,S-CDs, respectively.

For the purpose of measuring the fluorescence intensity (FI), a volume of 700 μL of the N,S-CDs working solution was added to a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks. Afterwards, 1000 μL of BRB (pH 12.0) was added, followed by the gradual addition of increasing concentrations of the NFZ working solution. The solutions were carefully mixed after the final volumes were completed using distilled water. Fluorescence (F) was recorded at λ ex/em of 320/420 nm, respectively. NFZ was omitted for the blank reading measurement (F
O
). Each experiment was conducted in triplicate. Ln (F
O
/F) versus the concentration of NFZ (μM) was plotted for obtaining the calibration curve. The regression equation was derived using the obtained calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for determination of NFZ in simulated gastric juice (SGJ)
ce:section.ce:para: SGJ was prepared following the procedures reported in the USP [41] by transferring 0.32 g pepsin, 0.2 g NaCl, and 0.7 mL HCl into 100 mL measuring flask and making up the final volume with distilled water. Then, in a 50 mL volumetric flask, 1 mL from the standard solution of NFZ (10 mM) was added and the final volume was completed with the SGJ. The whole content was well mixed. Different volumes from the SGJ containing NFZ have been withdrawn and filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filter into a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks containing 700 μL of the N,S-CDs working solution and 1000 μL of BRB (pH 12.0). The procedure outlined in section 2.6.1 was followed to construct the calibration graph of NFZ in SGJ and the regression equation has been calculated.

For capsules analysis, the contents of ten capsules were carefully emptied, weighed, and well mixed. Subsequently, a quantity of powder equivalent to 13.76 mg of NFZ was put into a 50 mL volumetric flask. To carry out suspension analysis, a specific volume of the oral suspension, containing 13.76 mg of NFZ, was accurately transferred into another 50 mL volumetric flask. Following that, a volume of 25 mL of methanol was introduced into each of the two flasks containing prepared capsules and suspension solution. The two flasks underwent sonication for 30 min. Afterwards, methanol was used to complete the final volume of each flask to achieve a final concentration of 1 mM for both NFZ capsules and suspension preparations. Contents of each flask were filtered, and 5 mL of both the capsules and suspension filtrate were put into two distinct 25 mL volumetric flasks. Distilled water was added to both flasks for completion to the final volumes, resulting in a final concentration of 0.2 mM for both NFZ capsules and suspension. Different volumes from each solution have been transferred into two series of 10-mL volumetric flasks followed by performing the steps outlined in section 2.6.1. The concentration of NFZ in both capsules and suspension formulations have been calculated using the regression equation. The % recoveries and their corresponding standard deviations were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alfalfa-derived N,S-CDs via microwave aided synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Since the era of microwave-aided synthesis began, it has found diverse applications in many fields. The efficient microwave-assisted reactions are attributed to absorption of microwave radiation by polar molecules and transforming it into heat, by dielectric heating [42]. Microwave-aided syntheses put substantial benefits over traditional hydrothermal techniques. This is represented in the extra rapidness, simplicity, and eco-friendliness [43]. In turn, microwave aided synthesis has been efficiently employed for the synthesis of CDs [25,44,45].

In this study, the implemented approach for microwave-assisted synthesis of N,S-CDs facilitated the accomplishment of the entire synthesis within few minutes by utilizing alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) as a cost-effective, naturally available, inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and sustainable carbon and nitrogen source. This was achieved by subjecting the alfalfa paste to low-energy (700 W) microwave irradiation. The developed method exhibited a notable green and economic characteristic as alfalfa is a globally abundant feedstock for animals. Reported phytochemical analysis of alfalfa reveals that the moisture, protein, fat, fiber, and ash contents in the green forage are: 80, 5.2, 0.5, 3.5 and 2.4 g/100 g [46]. Alfalfa is reported to contain a variety of phytochemical compounds such as amino acids (serine, histidine, phenylalanine, methionine, aspartic acid, tyrosine, glutamic acid, asparagine, alanine, and threonine), alkaloids (asparagines, trigoneline, stachydrine, and l-homostachydrine), coumarins (myrsellinol, esculetin, scopoletin, and 4-coumaric acid), flavonoids (quercetin, chrysoeriol, myricetin, luteolin, and apigenin), and saponins (e.g. soyasapogenols, hederagenin, and medicagenic acid) [21]. The preceding information supports the promising applicability of the designed methodology for preparation of self-doped N,S-CDs.

To enhance the efficiency of the synthesis process, a series of experiments were done. The richness of alfalfa green forage in moisture (80 %) [46] renders it easy to convert it into a paste without the addition of water by just grinding the alfalfa biomass with a home mixer. Initially, six experiments were conducted involving the exposure of 10 g of alfalfa paste to varying durations of microwave irradiation from 5 to 16 min to evaluate the influence of microwave time on the quantum yield of the prepared CDs. The experimental results indicated that a duration of 14 min is the optimal time, as it yielded the greatest fluorescence quantum yield (2.2 %) as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The synthesis procedures are delineated in Fig. 1.

For structural characterization, FTIR analysis was performed in order to investigate the structural profile and functional groups contained in the synthesized N,S-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the N,S-CDs demonstrate a prominent broad peak at 3286 cm−1, which could be assigned to O–H or N–H stretching vibration, a significant narrow peak at 1643 cm−1 is observed, which is associated with CC, CO, or CN stretching vibration, and the broad peak localized at 655 cm−1 refers to S–S,C–S–C, or C–S stretching vibration [47]. Furthermore, EDX analysis was adopted to confirm the composition of the N,S-CDs by determining types and relative proportions of atoms present in the synthesized sensor. The results depicted in Fig. 3B confirmed that the synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited a composition consisting of carbon (49.05 %), nitrogen (23.94 %), oxygen (26.18 %), and sulfur (0.83 %). These results suggest an efficient and considerable doping level. The doping of CDs with diverse functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur enhanced their polarity and solubility in aqueous environments. For zeta potential (ζ) measurement, 3000 μL From the N,S-CDs working solution was transferred to 10 mL volumetric flask and the final volume was completed with distilled water, triplicate measurements were done. The ζ was found to be −24.5 ± 6.63 mV, indicating a significant electrostatic repulsion between particles that is attributed to a high surface negative charge (Fig. 3C). As a result, the N,S-CDs exhibited a high level of stability, as the repulsion prevented their aggregation and maintained their size in the nano range [48].
TEM imaging was applied to explore the dimensions and morphology of N,S-CDs. The obtained images reveal spherical particles (Fig. 3D) with an average size of 4.01 ± 1.2 nm.

Furthermore, the optical characteristics of N,S-CDs were elucidated using UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The measurements were carried out in the working pH (BRB pH 12.0). The UV–Visible spectrum of the synthesized N,S-CDs (Fig. 4 A) displays an absorption band at 210 nm that is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic system core. A very slight broad shoulder around 250 nm refers to the n- π* transition of the carbonyl group [49]. Upon being excited at 320 nm, the N,S-CDs exhibit a clearly recognizable fluorescence emission at 420 nm (Fig. 4A). The emission of fluorescence in CDs is ascribed to the presence of conjugated aromatic system as well as surface imperfections [50]. Defect sites refer to regions that lack a fully intact sp2 domain that lead to energy traps [51]. The emission of fluorescence in CDs is attributed to the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized domains, as well as functionalized surface defects [52]. Furthermore, the implementation of functionalization and surface passivation techniques on CDs results in improved stabilization of surface imperfections. Consequently, this phenomenon enhances the effective radiative recombination of electron/hole pairs that are confined to the surface [50].

The photoluminescent properties of the produced N,S-CDs are reliant on the excitation wavelength. As demonstrated in Fig. 4B, the emission wavelength exhibits minimal variation while the excitation wavelength ranges from 300 to 340 nm. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a gradual increase up to 340 nm. In contrast, a change in the excitation wavelength from 350 to 400 nm results in a red shift of the maximum emission wavelength, accompanied by a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. The observed variation in behavior, which is dependent on excitation, could be attributed to the interaction between the surface functional groups and the deformed sp2 carbon arrangement of the N,S-CDs [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of formation of N,S-CDs by the developed synthesis method
ce:section.ce:para: A possible mechanism for CDs formation from alfalfa biomass via microwave irradiation was anticipated. Firstly, microwave energy is directly absorbed by the substrate (alfalfa biomass paste) causing direct molecular activation or localized superheating of the molecules. Then, dehydration of alfalfa paste precursor takes place causing decomposition of active constituents (amino acids, flavonoids, saponins, coumarins, etc.). Following this, decomposed alfalfa components underwent mild condensation forming large nanoparticles. The size of the nanoparticles then declined because of the continued intramolecular dehydration throughout microwave irradiation [54]. In next stage, C–C bonds and aromatic functional groups are created. This stage is followed by nucleation via diffusion of the aromatic moiety on the surface of particles [55]. Passivation of different functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, amine, etc.) takes place also at this stage. The surface passivation constitutes a protecting layer that stabilizes the formed CDs [56]. Over time, nanoparticles tend to convert to CDs. In the final stage, tiny CDs with narrow size distributions are formed. The proposed formation mechanism aligned well with the reported mechanism by Papaioannou et al. [54], Arul & Sethuraman [55], and Balakrishnan et al. [57].

The durability of luminous materials is a crucial determinant in the utilization of CDs as luminescent sensor. The investigation of the impact of pH on FI of N,S-CDs has been conducted throughout a pH range of 2.0–12.0, employing BRB. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, some reduction in fluorescence intensity is found primarily when the pH falls below 3.0 or exceeds 10.0. The synthesized N,S-CDs possesses the advantageous characteristic of maintaining a high and consistent FI across a broad range of pH values. The pH-reliant behavior is documented in the literature, and it has been extensively illustrated and analyzed through several mechanisms. The drop in fluorescence observed at pH values below 3.0 can be related to the protonation of the amino group located on the surface of N,S-CDs. This protonation process promotes the intramolecular electron transport between the protonated amino group and CDs, ultimately leading to a reduction in the fluorescence. Conversely, when the pH exceeds 10.0, the excited electrons within N,S-CDs could switch from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. The higher energy level of the HOMO of NH2 relative to that of N,S-CDs results in the potential for ground state electrons in the amino group to shift from their original position to the HOMO of N,S-CDs, leading to diminishing FI [58].

In addition, an examination was conducted to explore the photobleaching susceptibility of the N,S-CDs. This was achieved by exposing a solution of N,S-CDs to a continuous UV-radiation at 365 nm. A decrease in FI of only 10 % was recorded after 60 min. Furthermore, it is important to highlight that the N,S-CDs solution exhibits remarkable long-term stability, as seen by its consistent state without any observable indications of precipitation, flotation, or turbidity for more than 8 months. Besides, FI stability of the produced N,S-CDs was investigated over time. As shown in Fig. 4D, there was unremarkable change of FI of N,S-CDs for approximately 8 months with average FI 566, %RSD 3.72, and slight % decrease of FI by only about 9.17. Herein, the produced N,S-CDs exhibit remarkable stability, making them suitable for use as luminescent probes in the analysis of several different pharmaceuticals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method for N,S-CDs synthesis and some reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: The superiority of the proposed microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique over previously reported hydrothermal methods for CDs synthesis is apparent as demonstrated in Table 1 . The first and chief merit of the developed method over hydrothermal methods is its rapidness, where it is 20–100 times faster than reported hydrothermal synthesis methods [59–72]. The main reason for the rapidness of the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method is the straight coupling of microwaves to electric dipoles and dielectric and magnetic heat loss. The straightforward and powerful heat transport leads to quick completion of the reaction far from afar thermal equilibrium. This results in strongly reduced reaction time for CDs synthesis.

Moreover, considering the low-energy necessities as well as employing renewable, cheap, and widely existing alfalfa feedstock, the microwave hydrothermal synthesis of CDs is in excellent agreement with most of the green chemistry ideologies: use of harmless reactants and solvents, high competence regarding energy consumption set against yield, and minimized pollution. In contrary, hydrothermal methods [59–72] require high energy, very lengthy time to complete reaction, and use expensive chemical reactants. Despite some hydrothermal methods used plant parts like leaves and stems as sources for CDs synthesis [61,62,64–69], these require heating at very high temperatures (160–300 °C) for long times (3–12 h) to complete CDs synthesis.

It is noteworthy to highlight the economic efficiency, sustainability, and cheapness of alfalfa biomass as the carbon and dopant source in this study. The simplicity, economic efficiency, sustainability, low energy consumption, and rapid synthesis emphasize the outstanding characters of the proposed method and make it a favorable choice for different applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N,S-CDs as a fluorescent sensor for NFZ determination
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary investigations showed that the addition of NFZ to the N, S-CDs solution causes a substantial quenching of their inherent fluorescence as depicted in Fig. 5 A & B. No distinct color change has been observed in the presence of NFZ, except for a faint yellow color resulting from the native color of the drug (Fig. 5B). As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the FI emitted by N,S-CDs at 420 nm is gradually diminished when NFZ is added at increasing concentrations (ranging from 1.0 to 30.0 μM). Based on these findings, it was shown that approximately 50 % of the fluorescence emitted by N,S-CDs was suppressed in existence of 30.0 μM of NFZ.

To commence an inquiry into the underlying mechanism responsible for the observed decrease in fluorescence of N, S-CDs in the presence of NFZ, we have explored the potential contribution of the inner filter effect (IFE). The absorption spectrum of NFZ at pH of 12.0 demonstrates two discernible absorption peaks at 306 and 395 nm. These peaks exhibit an extensive overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs (Fig. 4A), suggesting the potential occurrence of primary and secondary IFE that are resulted when the quencher absorbs at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorescent substance, respectively [73]. In order to conduct a more in-depth analysis of the mechanism of quenching, FI of N,S-CDs in the existence of different concentrations of NFZ was corrected (F
corrected ) by subtracting the IFE to account for any interference caused by IFE using Eq. (2) [74]:

Subsequently, the calculation of the % suppressed efficiency (%E) for the corrected and observed FI was performed in accordance with Eq. (3) [75]:

The findings shown in Fig. 5C verified that IFE is the fundamental cause of fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs by NFZ. This is evident from the significant decrease in suppression efficiency by about 85 % when IFE is subtracted.

The quenching mechanism has been further supported by measuring FI at several temperature settings (303, 313, 323 K) and analyzing the results using the Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (4) [76]:
(4)

The Stern-Volmer quenching constant, symbolized as K
sv , and the molar concentration of NFZ, expressed as [NFZ]. Dynamic quenching is observed when the fluorophore in its excited state undergoes collision-induced deactivation with the quencher, leading to its return to the ground state. On the other hand, static quenching arises through the development of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the fluorophore and quencher. The value of K
sv exhibits a positive correlation with temperature in the context of dynamic quenching, but it demonstrates a negative correlation with temperature in the regard of static quenching. In contrast, the phenomenon of IFE does not exhibit characteristics of either static or dynamic quenching, and it is not reliant on temperature [77]. By conducting a graphical representation of F
0
/F versus [NFZ] at three different temperatures (303, 313, 323 K) as illustrated in Fig. 5D, it was noticed that the slope value (K
SV ) exhibits minimal fluctuations with changes in temperature and it is not temperature-reliant (Table 2 ). Hence, the exclusion of dynamic as well as static quenching occurrences, leaving the possibility of the quenching mechanism being IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of procedures for NFZ analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The highest sensitivity for the estimation of NFZ was attained through studying the influence of various experimental variables on the method, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . Initially, the pH effect was explored within the pH range of 2.0–12.0 using BRB. The study revealed that the F
0
/F ratio exhibited a significant drop within the pH range (2.0–7.0). Conversely, while over the alkaline pH, the F
0
/F ratio began to exhibit an increase, with the highest sensitivity observed at pH 12.0 Fig. 6A. The observed results can be correlated with the ionization of phenolic group of NFZ under basic conditions in addition to tautomeric configuration (Fig. 6B). The extended conjugation and conversion of nitro group to nitronates [78] initiate significant bathochromic shift (Fig. 6C). This resulted in a substantial increase of NFZ absorbance at 320 nm (λ ex of N,S-CDs) and 420 nm (λ em of N,S-CDs) as illustrated in Fig. 6C. This effect suggestively enhanced the IFE of NFZ, leading to about 2 folds improvement of the fluorescence quenching efficiency in alkaline pH. Consequently, the analytical determination of NFZ was conducted at pH 12.0 that yields the maximum sensitivity. The impact of buffer volume was illustrated by changing it within the range of 300–2500 μL. The lack of significant influence on the quenching efficiency can be observed in Fig. 6D when the buffer volume varied from 1000 to 2500 μL. Therefore, a buffer volume of 1000 μL was determined to be the optimal choice.

Besides, the volume of N,S-CDs was varied to explore its influence on the sensitivity of the assay. A range of 300–1000 μL was utilized for this purpose. The ideal volume of N,S-CDs for NFZ determination was selected to be 700 μL due to its maximum F
0
/F ratio, as depicted in Fig. 6D.

The selectivity of N,S-CDs was assessed to verify their suitability for the quantification of NFZ. FI of the sensor was measured in the existence of various co-existing chemicals that may exist as excipients in pharmaceutical preparations or that may occur in the biological fluids, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, SO4 2−, Mg2+, Cl−, Fe3+, Fe2+, carboxy methyl cellulose, sodium citrate, glucose, citric acid, and sucrose. Furthermore, the effect of a variety of co-administered drugs from different pharmacological categories, such as dexamethasone, paracetamol, spiramycin, and aceclofenac, on the FI of the developed N,S-CDs was examined. As illustrated in Table 3 , the presence of any of these materials doesn't have a significant effect on FI of the fluorescent sensor. The range of % change in FI varies from −6.6 % to +5.9 %, demonstrating the effectiveness and selectivity of the approach for pharmaceutical quality control and biological analysis.

The assessment of the proposed technique was carried out in compliance with the ICH guidelines [79]. In order to assess the efficacy of determination, the FI of the N,S-CDs was measured while varying the concentrations of NFZ. Ln F
0
/F exhibited a strong linear correlation with NFZ concentration within the range of 1.0–30.0 μM, as evidenced by the following regression equation:
(5) Ln (F
0
/F) = 0.0219 [NFZ] + 0.0109 (r = 0.9999)

The variable denoted as [NFZ] represents the concentration of nifuroxazide [μM], while the symbol r corresponds to the correlation coefficient. The values for the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were established using formulas 3.3σ/S and 10σ/S, respectively. Here, σ represents the standard deviation of the response, while S denotes calibration curve slope. LOD and LOQ were 0.16 and 0.49 μM, respectively, as shown in Table 4 . The obtained results demonstrate the probe's significant level of sensitivity.

The method's accuracy was assessed by conducting statistical comparison between the assay data obtained through the suggested approach and those obtained using a reported method [32] employing student t-test and variance ratio F-test [80]. The analysis determined the absence of statistically significant variation between the suggested and reported method [32], since the estimated t-test and F-test values were found to be lower than the tabulated ones [80] (Table 5 ).

In addition, the method's precision was assessed through analysis of three different concentrations of NFZ three times on the same day (intra-day precision) and over the course of three subsequent days (inter-day precision [79]. The approach demonstrates a high level of precision, as seen by the calculated % RSD being less than 2 % [80] (Table 6 ). The findings of the validation study indicate the effectiveness of the method that has been developed and its suitability for practical use in pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NFZ in SGJ by the alfalfa-derived N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A main property of NFZ is its poor absorption in the gastrointestinal tract, which confirms its localization against the pathogens and limits its efficacy to the zone of infection. Consequently, this antidiarrheic medication has no systematic action [81]. This fact has been confirmed by the published reports issued that no intact NFZ was detected in human plasma or urine after oral administration [82]. On the other hand, the study of Radovanović et al. revealed that the release profile of NFZ demonstrated a gradual and consistent increase over time in SGJ as after 60 min around 54 % of administered NFZ is released in the stomach [83]. This means that, following a single oral dose of 200 mg NFZ, about 100 mg will be existing in the stomach fluid. Considering the average volume of SGJ is 25 mL [84], it is anticipated that the concentration of NFZ in SGJ is approximately 15 mM. Thus, the achieved concentration range and LOD of the proposed method enables its application for NFZ at its localized site of action in SGJ. This investigation discloses the reliability and applicability of the developed probe for real application in this simulated biological matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NFZ in commercially available dosage forms by the alfalfa-derived N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The method adopted was assessed in terms of its reliability and effectiveness in determining NFZ in commercially available capsules and suspension formulations. The results obtained from the analysis of NFZ capsules and suspension indicate high % recoveries ranging from 98.29 to 99.44 and 98.88–101.81 %, respectively. Additionally, SD for NFZ capsules and suspension are relatively small, with values of ±0.61 and ± 1.54, respectively (Table 5). These results provide confirmation that the proposed method is effective and suitable for accurately determining NFZ concentration lacking any potential interference from prevalent excipients or additives. The findings from the analysis of NFZ in capsules and suspension were compared to the results acquired through the comparison method [32] using statistical tests such as the student t-test and variance ratio F-test [80]. The results indicated the lack of significant difference between the two methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method in relation to methods reported for NFZ analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The comparison between the suggested approach for determining NFZ and the existing methods in literature revealed numerous strengths and advantages. These findings suggest that the proposed method holds significant potential for appropriate use in the pharmaceutical area (Table 9 ). The reported spectrofluorimetric method [32] involve the alteration of the native structure of NFZ by means of reduction using Zinc dust, which is known to be toxic, in the presence of concentrated HCl, an acid known for its corrosive property. Additionally, it should be noted that the reaction is not performed in situ, requiring time optimization to ensure complete reduction. Furthermore, the spectrofluorimetric approach developed by El-Zaher and Mahrouse [33] entails the application of elevated temperatures up to 100 °C for evaporating the drug to dryness, as well as the utilization of hazardous and corrosive organic substances such as concentrated sulfuric acid and ethyl acetoacetate. Besides, the published spectrofluorimetric method [34] synthesized N,S-CDs from non-sustainable source (citric acid and l-cysteine) using a hydrothermal approach which requires high temperature (70 °C) and longer time (12 h) for NFZ analysis. In contrast, the proposed methodology circumvents the utilization of hazardous chemicals, exhibits low energy consumption, requires only 14 min for N,S-CDs synthesis from a sustainable source, relies on in situ mixing and analysis, and preserves the inherent structure of the pharmaceutical compound. The determination of NFZ was also previously conducted using spectrophotometric techniques [35–38]. However, the proposed method offers increased sensitivity and comprises straightforward processes. In addition, the reported TLC methods [35,38] are less sensitive than the proposed study and involving the use of hazardous organic solvents like acetone and chloroform. The HPLC approaches that have been reported for the quantification of NFZ [38,39] involve the use of potentially dangerous and costly substances such as acetonitrile. Moreover, the equilibration process and mobile phase preparation necessitate long time. Moreover, the sensitivity is lower. On the flip side, the suggested method offers expeditious procedures and employs harmless precursors and solvents.

Recently, there has been a significant focus on the advancement of environmentally friendly analytical techniques and the modification of existing methods to minimize the use of hazardous chemicals and decrease waste formation. This growing approach is referred to as green analytical chemistry [85]. The evident advantages of the developed analytical technique are readily apparent due to low-energy consumption, less waste generation, and efficient synthesis procedures, in addition to its reliance on renewable and highly safe carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source. In this particular context, the latest tool and software utilized for assessing the environmental sustainability of analytical methodologies, namely the complementary green analytical procedure index (Complex GAPI) [86] has been applied for assessment of the proposed method. This scoring system is primarily designed to assess analytical methods that involve the utilization of materials generated earlier to the analytical step. The result is depicted in the pictogram presented in Fig. 8 A. The hexagon located at the lower portion symbolizes the environmentally conscious nature of the pre-analysis practices. The process encompasses the assessment of several factors such as conditions, reagents, solvents, equipment, as well as the subsequent procedures for set up and refinement of the final products. The upper pentagonal fields pertain to the various components of the analytical technique, specifically the chemicals, procedures, and instrumentations. These components are evaluated based on several factors, including the quantity, health danger, safety risks of the chemicals and solvents, energy utilization, produced waste, and the Scope of the method (qualitative or quantitative). The green, yellow, and red colors are used to represent the low, medium, and high environmental consequences associated with each stage of the pre-analysis procedures and the method of analysis. The pictogram obtained (Fig. 8A) represents the level of environmental sustainability in both the synthesis process of N,S-CDs and the analytical method used. Furthermore, the application of the Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE) [87] has been employed to quantitatively assess the level of environmental sustainability of the suggested approach. This assessment involves an examination of many factors, including the amounts and hazards of reagents, generated waste, energy requirements, and the complexity of processes involved. The whole analysis yields a total score of 0.67 (Fig. 8B), which signifies the method's remarkable level of greenness.

The outcomes from both greenness criteria demonstrate complete concurrence, hence affirming the environmentally friendly nature of the suggested technique. The fluorescent probe under consideration relies on the utilization of N, S-CDs derived from sustainable plant-based source, which provide both environmental and human safety attributes. Additionally, the synthesis approach for N,S-CDs is conducted in a single step, with a significantly reduced time requirement and energy consumption compared to the other methods commonly used for N,S-CDs synthesis. The utilization of inexpensive and readily available plant materials, along with a standard household microwave oven, contributes to the cost-effectiveness of the suggested approach. The method's eco-friendliness is further strengthened by the minimal use of organic solvents and the straightforwardness of the analytical procedures, which relies on a mix-and-read approach. These parameters serve to enhance the green credentials of the developed analytical method and validate its use for NFZ analysis.

This work introduces the novel application of alfalfa biomass, which is readily accessible in agricultural settings, as a feedstock for the efficient and cost-effective production of N, S-CDs. This method is characterized by its simplicity, sensitivity, affordability, and rapidity. The production of N,S-CDs was achieved in a time frame of 14 min by a one-pot one-step carbonization process utilizing a conventional microwave, which required low energy and did not involve the use of any chemicals. A comprehensive characterization of the N, S-CDs was conducted using several techniques including TEM, EDX, ζ assessment, fluorescence analysis, UV–Vis, and IR spectroscopy. The N,S-CDs exhibit outstanding characteristics of water solubility and stability that afford them a significant potential for use as fluorescent sensors for pharmaceutical applications. The produced N,S-CDs were utilized as fluorescent nanoprobes to quantify the commonly used drug NFZ in capsules and suspension over the concentration range of 1.0–30.0 μM, yielding %recovery ±SD of 98.75 ± 0.61 and 100.63 ± 1.54, respectively. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs were efficiently applied for monitoring the concentration of NFZ in simulated gastric juice (SGJ) with % recovery 99.59 ± 1.4. These promising results validate the synthesized N,S-CDs for quality control of NFZ dosage forms as well as applications in drug release studies. The developed analytical technique demonstrated a strong emphasis on sustainability, as they relied on cost-effective, abundant, renewable, and non-toxic plant biomass. This method avoided the introduction of potentially dangerous organic solvents or toxic substances, while also minimizing energy consumption, time, and effort. Additionally, they implemented a streamlined synthesis technique that involves a single step, along with an analytical approach that involves mixing and reading.
